retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Chronic Mandibular Hypomobility
retrieved_docs.content: It is a long-term painless restriction of the mandible. Pain only occurs when using force to attempt opening beyond limitations. Hypomobility can be caused by ankylosis, muscle contracture, or coronoid process impedance.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The TMJ performs complex movements across multiple orthogonal planes and axes of rotation, functioning synergistically with the other components (see Image . Temporomandibular Joint). Coordination with the contralateral TMJ ensures synchronized movement. Dysfunction or pathology of this structure can lead to pain, limited jaw movement, and impaired quality of life. Medical management focuses on diagnosing disorders such as TMJ dysfunction, arthritis, and trauma. Surgical intervention may be necessary for severe joint degeneration, ankylosis, tumors, or congenital anomalies.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intraarticular disorders refer to inflammatory or mechanical factors that affect the joint itself, articular disc displacement being the most common. [1] Other intraarticular causes include trauma, capsular inflammation, osteoarthritis, hypermobility, and inflammatory diseases, like rheumatoid arthritis. It is not clear yet if malocclusion contributes to TMD. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: TMD symptoms can appear at any age, but a peak incidence occurs in adults between 20 to 40 years. [1] [5] Women are much more likely to be affected than men, the reason for which is still unknown. [1] Even though up to 60 to 70% of the population shows signs of TMJ disorders, only 5% to 12% of people report symptoms and require treatment. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Adherences and Adhesions
retrieved_docs.content: An adherence represents a brief hold of the articular surfaces. Adhesion can happen between the condyle and disc or the disc and fossa. Adhesions result from a fibrous connective tissue or loss of lubrication between the structures. It is characterized by restriction in the normal translation of the condyle movement with no pain. In chronic situations, the patient senses an inability to get the teeth back to occlusion during the closure.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of TMD is mildly understood, but it is believed to be multifactorial; the appropriate management of the condition requires recognizing the predisposing and contributing factors. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Systemic Arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: Several arthritides can affect the TMJ, including traumatic arthritis, infectious arthritis, and rheumatoid arthritis.

retrieved_docs.title: Trismus -- Etiology -- Inflammatory
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoarthritis Soft tissue fibrosis TMJ ankylosis Rheumatoid arthritis Scleroderma Temporal arteritis

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Osteoarthrosis
retrieved_docs.content: Arthrosis is the adaptive unaltered arthritic changes of the bone due to decreased bone loading. It occurs after the overloading of the joint, mainly due to parafunctional activity, and is often associated with disc dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Structural Incompatibility with Articular Surfaces
retrieved_docs.content: The disorder results from changes in the smooth sliding surfaces of the TMJ. The alteration causes friction stickiness and inhibits joint function. Structural incompatibility classifies as a deviation in form, adhesions, subluxation, and spontaneous dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Resorption and ankylosis are associated more with the use of rigid splints as opposed to semi-rigid ones. Ankylosis can be especially problematic in young patients going through the facial growing phase as the surrounding structures will continue to develop, and the tooth will look submerged.

retrieved_docs.title: Trismus -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Since trismus has been defined as a restriction in mouth opening regardless of etiology, it does not have a wide differential diagnosis. Some authors have argued that "true trismus" is mediated via the trigeminal nerve, and intraarticular causes of impaired mouth opening, such as TMJ ankylosis or fibrosis, should be considered separately. [1] Others have outlined such intraarticular processes as subclassifications of trismus. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Temporomandibular Joint -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple pathologies can affect the TMJ and result in varying degrees of clinical dysfunction. Rheumatoid arthritis may present with crepitus, limited range of motion (ROM), increased stiffness, pain, and joint sounds. Psoriatic arthropathy often manifests as joint sounds, morning stiffness, and pain. Ankylosing spondylitis can cause joint sounds, pain in the lateral pterygoid muscles, hypertrophy of the masseter, and ROM restriction. Pain originating from the joint may be associated with articular sounds. Fibromyalgia syndrome can produce myalgia and generalized TMJ pain. Muscular pain may also contribute to dysfunction. Multiple sclerosis may be associated with myalgia of the TMJ and pain during mandibular movement. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Up to 40% of patients report the remission of symptoms without any intervention, [26] and most patients have a favorable response to conservative treatment. [1] A small number of patients develop refractory or persistent TMD. There are no known risk factors associated with chronic TMD. But, recently published data correlated heightened sympathetic tone with chronic TMJ pain. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Temporomandibular Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: Pain originates in the joint, and features of inflammation or infection over the affected joint are usually seen, such as edema, erythema, or increased temperature. Further symptoms include dental occlusal changes, e.g., ipsilateral posterior open bite if intraarticular with unilateral swelling or effusion. This disorder is also known as synovitis or capsulitis, although these terms limit the sites of nociception. TMD is a localized condition; there should be no history of systemic inflammatory disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Fibrodysplasia Ossificans Progressiva -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Common complications associated with this disease include respiratory system infection, joint disabilities, cardiopulmonary complications (thoracic insufficiency syndrome, right-side congestive heart failure), submandibular swelling, temporomandibular joint ankylosis conductive loss of hearing. [5] [6] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2579
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine and/or metabolic disorder is classified within the broader category of other non‑traumatic joint disorders. These other non‑traumatic joint disorders are directly related to the condition described in Arabic as اعتلال مفصلي (joint disease). Recognizing that endocrine or metabolic‑related arthropathy maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and shares a relationship with اعتلال مفصلي helps clinicians place these conditions within a unified framework of joint pathology, facilitating more comprehensive assessment and management of patients with non‑traumatic joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: pathologies, entrapment syndromes, headache and facial‑nerve disorders, musculoskeletal pathology and diagnostic imaging, thereby supporting comprehensive patient assessment, precise diagnosis and targeted clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2851

This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing various non‑traumatic joint pathologies as part of the overall spectrum of اعتلال مفصلي for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 983

These specific spinal and disc pathologies are therefore understood as manifestations of the broader bone‑centered disease entity, allowing clinicians to classify and address them within a unified diagnostic and therapeutic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine AND/OR metabolic disorder is classified under other non‑traumatic joint disorders. This arthropathy has its primary finding site in the joints (klouby) and is linked to the broader concept of joint disease (اعتلال مفصلي). Recognizing that this condition maps to other non‑traumatic joint disorders and involves the joints helps clinicians understand how endocrine or metabolic disturbances can produce non‑traumatic arthropathies that belong to the overall spectrum of joint disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2430
retrieved_docs.content: Other miscellaneous procedures are classified as a subset of other nonoperative procedures, indicating that they fall under the broader category of nonoperative interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: الالْتِهاب (inflammation) is associated with other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities, indicating that inflammatory processes can contribute to the development or worsening of skeletal disorders and structural abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as addressing underlying inflammation may help prevent or mitigate bone pathology and deformities, thereby improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1922
retrieved_docs.content: Arthropathy associated with an endocrine and/or metabolic disorder is linked to the entity “nemoc” and is classified under the category “other non‑traumatic joint disorders.” The group “other non‑traumatic joint disorders” is further connected to the Arabic term “اعتلال مفصلي,” which denotes joint disease. Together, these relationships indicate that arthropathy arising from endocrine or metabolic disturbances is considered a subset of non‑traumatic joint disorders and is conceptually related to the broader notion of joint disease, providing a framework for understanding its classification and clinical context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.
